title: Lightweight difficulty/discrimination calibration from voluntary aggregate response data
context: The project disclaims high-stakes psychometric calibration (IRT/equating) but aggregate, de-identified voluntary response data — if an adoption partner agrees to share it — could provide lightweight difficulty and discrimination signals to improve item quality without any individual-level data. This research task investigates whether such calibration is feasible, defines the data requirements (aggregate/de-identified only, never high-stakes calibration claims, partner consent required), and proposes a methodology if viable.
objective: Research whether lightweight difficulty and discrimination calibration is feasible from voluntary aggregate response data, define the minimum data requirements, and produce a methodology proposal if viable.
acceptanceCriteria: Research reviews existing open calibration approaches (classical test theory, lightweight IRT proxies) applicable to small, voluntary aggregate datasets.
Data requirements documented: aggregate/de-identified only; individual response data never collected or stored; partner explicit consent required; no high-stakes calibration claims made.

If viable: a proposed methodology document including sample-size requirements, statistical approach, and how results would annotate item difficulty fields — framed as informational signals, not validated calibration.
If not viable: a clear rationale for why the project should not attempt calibration at its current scale.
Document is CC-BY-4.0 licensed; commit is DCO signed-off.
resources: Classical test theory literature (item difficulty, item-total correlation)
quiz-bank-open-reuse-020 (partner relationships — data-sharing consent would come through partners)
output: A committed research document on lightweight calibration feasibility, data requirements, and a methodology proposal (or rationale for deferral), framed as informational signals only.
